description: First 90 days after incarceration: ID recovery without ID, Medicaid reactivation, SNAP eligibility by state, voting rights restoration, parole/probation basics, and employment resources.
sources: SSA Program Operations Manual System (POMS), SI 00501.420
USDA SNAP eligibility: 7 CFR 273.11, food and nutrition service policy
National Conference of State Legislatures voting rights tracker (2025)
HHS guidance on Medicaid suspension vs. termination (CMCS Info Bulletin 2014)
EEOC guidance on criminal record background checks (2012, updated 2024)

triples.subject: ID without ID problem
triples.object: obtain birth certificate first using any documents you have (incarceration records accepted by most vital records offices); then use birth certificate plus incarceration release papers to get state ID

triples.subject: birth certificate
triples.object: VitalChek.com (online, $10–$40); or contact state vital records office; many DOCs assist before release — ask unit case manager at least 60 days out

triples.subject: Social Security card
triples.object: free from SSA (ssa.gov/number-card); need identity document (state ID or passport) and proof of citizenship; can apply online in most states if SSN is already on file

triples.subject: state ID fee waivers
triples.object: most states offer free or reduced-fee IDs for recently released individuals; ask DMV specifically — some require proof of release within 30–180 days; DC, NY, IL, CA have robust waiver programs

triples.subject: Medicaid suspension vs. termination
triples.object: federal law requires states to suspend (not terminate) Medicaid during incarceration for most individuals; suspended Medicaid reactivates faster on release than a new application

triples.subject: Medicaid reactivation
triples.object: if suspended: coverage can resume within 1–7 days of release if state systems work correctly; if terminated: reapplication required — processing up to 45 days (expedited if homeless)

triples.subject: Medicaid reactivation
triples.object: contact state Medicaid office on day of release with ID and release paperwork; many correctional facilities can start paperwork pre-release — check with case manager

triples.subject: ACA Medicaid expansion
triples.object: in the 40 expansion states: returning citizens without dependent children who were previously ineligible may qualify for first time; income cutoff approximately $21,000/year (2025)

triples.subject: SNAP eligibility with felony drug conviction
triples.object: 1996 welfare law created lifetime SNAP ban for drug felony convictions; BUT most states have opted out partially or fully — check your state

triples.subject: SNAP drug felony ban opt-outs
triples.object: CA, CO, CT, IL, MD, MA, ME, MI, MN, NY, OH, OR, PA, RI, VT, WA, WI, and others have fully eliminated the ban; check current USDA FNS state options table

triples.subject: SNAP drug felony ban opt-outs
triples.object: TX, FL, GA, NC, and others allow SNAP if enrolled in drug treatment program or after waiting period; some require compliance with probation/parole

triples.subject: SNAP application timeline
triples.object: 30-day processing; 7-day expedited if income under $150/month and liquid assets under $100, or shelter costs exceed income — request expedited explicitly at application

triples.subject: voting rights restoration
triples.object: rights restore automatically on release in ME, VT (never lose them — can vote from prison), plus 21 other states; others require probation/parole completion or waiting period

triples.subject: voting rights restoration
triples.object: CA, CO, CT, HI, IL, MD, MA, MI, NV, NH, NJ, NY (2021 law), OR, RI, WA restore voting rights automatically upon release from incarceration

triples.subject: voting rights restoration
triples.object: FL (Amendment 4, 2018, but requires completion of full sentence including fines/fees), TX, GA, PA, OH, NC restore after completing all supervision

triples.subject: voting rights restoration
triples.object: IA and a few others require governor pardon or formal petition process; TN and VA have varying requirements — check with state board of elections for current rules

triples.subject: parole compliance basics
triples.object: report to PO within 24–72 hours of release (varies by state), notify of address/employment changes, no new arrests, no weapons, no leaving jurisdiction without permission

triples.subject: parole violation
triples.object: technical violations (missed meeting, failed drug test) often result in sanctions rather than reincarceration under Second Chance Act guidance — you have right to hearing before revocation

triples.subject: probation fees
triples.object: many states charge supervision fees ($30–$100/month); inability to pay is not automatic violation if you're making good-faith effort — document inability; some states have suspended fees

triples.subject: employment with criminal record
triples.object: employers cannot categorically exclude all people with criminal records — must consider nature of offense, time elapsed, and job relevance (Green factors); blanket bans may violate Title VII

triples.subject: ban-the-box laws
triples.object: 37 states and 150+ cities/counties have ban-the-box laws delaying criminal history question until later in hiring; federal contractors covered by Executive Order 13672 equivalent policies

triples.subject: federal bonding program
triples.object: free fidelity bonds ($5,000 coverage, 6 months) to employers hiring high-risk job applicants including people with criminal records; administered through state workforce agencies

triples.subject: Work Opportunity Tax Credit (WOTC)
triples.object: employers get $2,400–$9,600 federal tax credit for hiring ex-felons within 1 year of conviction or release; mention this to potential employers — it reduces their cost of hiring you

triples.subject: reentry housing
triples.object: PHAs can admit people with criminal records to public housing unless conviction is for methamphetamine production on premises or sex offender registration — other denials are discretionary

triples.subject: reentry housing
triples.object: lifetime ban for sex offenders on registry; 3-year ban after drug eviction; other convictions are discretionary — appeal denials using One Strike guidance; ask for reasonable accommodation

triples.subject: reentry resources
triples.object: re-entry.net and reentrycouncil.org provide state-by-state resource directories; 211 hotline connects to local services for housing, food, and benefits in most areas
